speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 11, 1996. Renate Rossmere Loeser, L-O-E-S-E-R, is being interviewed by Diane Weinreich in Rye Brook, New York, in the USA. The interview was being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Diane Weinreich, W-E-I-N-R-E-I-C-H. Today is July-- is June 11, 1996. I am interviewing Renate Loeser Rossmere in Rye Brook, New York, USA. The interview is being conducted in English. Can you reach me from there? Will you be able to or no?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, let us begin this interview. Please introduce yourself, tell us your name at birth, and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Renate Loeser, L-O-E-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Rossmere, R-O-S-S-M-E-R-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's March 6, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany. Would you tell us please your very earliest memories, who lived in your family, who comprised your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family consisted of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Kate Loeser. My father, Oscar Loeser. And my brother, Oswald Loeser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was five years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant. He was also a partner in a chemical firm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life was very comfortable. I had a very nice childhood. I had very loving parents, parents who loved each other. And so it was a very good family life. It was-- I cannot think of any incidents that, that marred that my life at that point. I had a lovely childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your extended family? Who live around you or near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents lived about 15, 20 minutes away. It was-- people rarely had cars at that point. So when we visited, we walked to them. And I remember many times walking with my mother to visit the grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those were your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maternal grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maternal. And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my paternal-- sorry. Agnes and Richard Shaeffer-- S-C-H-A-E-F-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived five minutes from my maternal grandparents. They lived in the back of them. And their name, my grandmother's name was Marta Loeser. And her husband was Emil Loeser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get together for family holidays, family celebrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did. My maternal grandparents were quite social and gave many parties, and I remember well. It was a lot of fun we had at those parties, even though I was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Judaism celebrated in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid it wasn't celebrated at all. We got brought up totally irreligious and barely knew that we were Jewish. We knew we were Jewish, but in no way did we celebrate the holidays. We celebrated Christmas as a family holiday, and that was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my parents telling us. We were-- and later on, of course, through Hitler, we became, I became more aware of it. But at the time, there was very little talk about religion, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to be Jewish then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It meant we're just like everybody else. And it really didn't mean very much to me, I'm sorry to say, because, simply because there was no religion in, in our household. We were-- the important thing was to be a good human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was true from both your maternal and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paternal side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paternal, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My maternal grandmother, I think, came from a very religious household. I think she was born in Denmark. But there was very little talk about that. So I know very little about it, nothing really.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't celebrate anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even coming from a very religious home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who was Jewish and who was not Jewish? Did that come into play when you [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did when I was about eight, nine-- nine years old. And simply because I had a girlfriend who lived in the same floor we did in our apartment building. And she was Christian. And they were a large family of lovely people. And they moved away into the suburbs of Hamburg and [GERMAN] And when I visited there once, several times, I became very much aware of that I was somehow different than they were. For one thing, their servants all commented on the wonderful hair I had, which was totally different than my friends, who was very blonde and had straight hair. And mine was extremely curly and dark brown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was wondering at that point why was she pointing out my hair so? What were-- why was it so different? I'd never thought of it before. And later on, I realized why she had done that. And there was a lot of talk at the dinner table about Jews and Christians, so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the worst of it was that in the neighborhood there was a group of teenage boys. And when they heard that I came to visit the family, they threatened me-- to beat me up. And there was a lovely festival where you walk with a lantern, which has a light in it, a candle in it. And you walk at night in the procession through the neighborhood and sing songs, and so on. And they said that when we-- indeed this was going to take place and we were going to walk through the woods with our lanterns that they were going to come and beat me up. And I was very frightened by that because I was Jewish. And I was very frightened by that, but I didn't tell it to anybody that this was so. My girlfriend knew it, but didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wasn't going to wait till this happened, and so I faked a terrible stomachache. And so much so that the doctor came and couldn't find anything wrong, naturally. But my friend's mother thought it better to send me home because of the stomachache, and so I escaped the beating up, which I might or not have gotten. But it was very scary. And that, for the first time, made me aware that indeed I was different than others, than this good friend of mine, perhaps. Did it end your relationship with this friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it kind of petered out. For one thing, she had moved away. But then several years later, maybe two years later, we met her in the city. And we were-- my mother and I were shopping for clothes. And we met this friend and she pretended she did not know me anymore. It was kind of devastating.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not happening to you however in Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the incident where the boy was going to beat you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was outside of Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was outside, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, where they lived then. They moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that-- was that different in small towns? Was the-- was the anti-Semitism more pronounced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just in your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my experience, that's where it had seemed to be more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of your father being-- being pointed out as a Jewish merchant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not until the Kristallnacht. Before that, his business was also in the suburbs of Hamburg, in [GERMAN] And you had to take a train in order to get there. So I don't-- as far as I know, I don't think he encountered any unpleasantnesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your schooling like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that night, my parents sent me to a public school so that I would meet everybody and be educated together with everybody. And in the later years, in '33, I noticed that some of the boys were wearing a uniform, a Hitler Youth uniform, and that they wear absent a lot from school because they had to participate in the Hitler Youth meetings. But there was basically no anti-Semitism at that point. I didn't feel in any way shut out. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not by the teachers or the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the teacher was wonderful. He was a young new teacher who was very, very lovely. His name was Herr Puck, P-U-C-K. And we all adored him, boys and girls. That was a totally mixed class. And he handled it, seemed to have handled it very well. So that, that-- but that was the, really the very first awareness of, of the Hitler Youth and things to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid of-- were you afraid of these young boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a good question. Maybe a little bit, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jewish children in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any discussion among you about these boys or about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember. There may have been. But I-- I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like a secret burden that you carried with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps a little bit, yes. Yes, it was something you really didn't talk about very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it spoken of in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much actually. I think my parents talked more amongst themselves about what was happening. And at the dinner table, I heard the words of, you know, what might be difficult times ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, do you remember any specifics?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Very little, really, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- as the years progressed, did it restrict your, your travel or your activities in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the very end, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but gradually.

speaker: interviewee
text: But gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was 10, I was sent to a private girl school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In order to get a better education than I had received up to then. And-- and this school closed in 1937 because of so many Jewish children-- girls-- having left and immigrated to other countries that they couldn't keep it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was not exclusively for Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't exclusively, but there was a great many. The percentage was fairly high of Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware that people were leaving the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: And aware that they were doing so because they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And there were other things. We had different religions instruction. I had to go to the temple for my religious instructions, while the Christian girls received theirs in school. One day, I remember we were-- the school closed because Hitler was going to visit Hamburg and was coming past, riding past our school. And so the school closed in order to make it possible for everyone to view this. And the Jewish girls were sent home because they weren't supposed to participate. But I decided that I wanted to see what this was all about. And so I stayed and watched him coming past in the car, standing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just saw a person standing up in the car, raising his hands, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were you away from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far away. It's hard-- hard to remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you terrified?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I could see him quite, quite clearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you terrified what this person was bringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit because of the importance this all had, the whole school closing and everybody out there watching this person, this one person, coming by and what would he do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there discussion in your school about, about Hitler, about, about his ideas?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none. As a matter of fact, the teacher, my last year's teacher, was a very, was a woman who was a Nazi, really. And so there never was any discussion. The less discussion, the better. She taught us, but we did not know of her. I mean, we knew of her private feelings. But it was not mentioned in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your feeling about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's a difficult question. Because there was so little from home that made us feel really Jewish, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a confusion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps. More confusion than definite feelings of being Jewish. It felt good being in the religious instructions in the temple and going to attending some of the holidays. But it was as being in a vacuum because my parents weren't there, my parents never set foot in a, in a synagogue or a temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- my brother didn't. So it was just me being there, somewhere, learning something that the others hadn't learned or didn't know very much about. Why then did your parents send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their idea was to offer us the possibilities of choosing the religion we wanted to when-- once we were old enough to do so. And so they wanted to show me what the Jewish religion was like, and so thereby had me take the religious instruction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the religious instruction was about? Was it historical?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about the Bible, yes, and about the holy holidays-- some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an effort to instill pride in, or was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so because we viewed this as being a little bit of a nuisance, also, you see. I mean, to go there on a Saturday afternoon or Saturday morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said you felt something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, on the other hand, I also felt a little bit of a waste of time, because I think it is very important that it's encouraged at home and talked about, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you then in that all-girl's school? You said it closed in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there from 10 till 13, 12 or 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had one year of private tutoring with some other children whose school had closed, some of my own classmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish, yes. And that was wonderful. I learned more in that year than I did up to then in the entire school year, I think. It was just marvelous. We had various teachers and very dedicated, very knowledgeable people. It was marvelous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those Jewish teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then your life really became much more restricted and closed, didn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And defined to a Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. And at home we were not allowed to have any non-Jewish servants and had to be very careful in that. And we were not allowed to sit on park benches, I remember, not allowed to talk to anyone who's not Jewish. It became quite restricted. But it was-- yes, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I was just going to ask then about your father's business. Then he must have been affected by, by this-- by this growing set of rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm sure he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was not discussed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not discussed, or it was discussed in the privacy of [? that. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what about with your grandparents? How are they adjusting? How are they reacting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the same with them. My grandpa-- father-- the factory belonged to him. And so he was affected by it just the same way my father was. And that all stopped on the 11th of November during the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about your experiences with Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my experience on the night was nothing. I went to sleep and didn't know about anything. But the next morning when my father got out of the train at the station in [GERMAN], he was taken by SS people and incarcerated in a concentration camp, together was two other partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one was my uncle, maternal uncle, Erwin Shaeffer. And one was a Mr. Levy, and he was the engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the concentration camp they were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Oranienburg. It with near Berlin. And then that of course was a devastating moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was he there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there three or four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get word that he was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my mother-- got word. How, I don't know. She talked to us. And she mainly talked to us in the-- I remember-- in the bathroom and while the water was running. See, she was very afraid that people would hear. And so that was the way she was communicating with us. We always had to come to the bathroom. I remember the water was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with regard to the increase of the Nuremberg Laws and that Jews weren't allowed to have any money and have to pay millions of dollars and all this, that was imparted to us in the bathroom. They-- they needed millions of dollars to get your father out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Jews, period, had to pay millions of dollars-- millions of marks, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: For, for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the government, just because they're Jewish. They weren't allowed to have any money. The condition to get my father out, I believe, was that he could leave the country at that point. That was still possible. And so I-- my mother's sister lived in England and was able to get us all, save all our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lotte Elkon, E-L-K-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: She lived in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in London, yes, in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, how was she able to get you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: She vouched for all of us. She gave us the affidavits. And first she vouched my brother and myself so that we could come over. It was a children's transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, what month and year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was December 1938. It was the 15th of December, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had been out for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was still interned, he was still away, incarcerated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what kind of treatment he was getting in the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, none.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he never told you about it afterward?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never told me about it. No, he talked a little bit to my brother but never to my mother and myself about it. It must have been devastating [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you see, my mother decided to send us away when-- because she just didn't know what was going to happen after my father was taken by the Nazis. And I think it was a very courageous move on her part and must have been tearing her heart to pieces because I know what it would feel like if I'd had to send my children away. I was 14 and my brother was 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of preparations? How long-- how long did your, did the planning take.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. You see, the 11th of November was Kristallnacht. And we were shipped away on the 15th of December. So it's barely, it's a little bit over a month. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- excuse me, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That she prepared us for this move. It was organized by a Jewish organization who organized the Kindertransport. Don't ask me for the name, I'm not quite sure of it. And you had to apply for this, and, I imagine, pay quite a bit of money. I not-- wasn't sure of the details. I Was 14, you really are still very immature and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was in the preparation, the emotional preparing that your mother gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to say that we would live with my aunt, that life would be a lot different, and to try and make life as easy as possible for her and be good children, in other words. And that they would try and return as soon as possible and come that way too as soon as possible, as soon as my father would be released. But of course, nobody knew when that would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or if that would be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or if that would be. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the transport like? How many children left with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of children did. The evening before we left, of course, a lot of relatives and friends came to say goodbye and brought lovely presents, which then we couldn't take along because we were only allowed to a little suitcase to take with us. And as far as I know, it was only one-- one mark or 10 marks, probably 10 marks. I remember one mark, but I think that would be too little. It could have been. And on the morning of the transport when we were supposed to go to the station, my brother had a terrible stomachache. And so wife my mother called a doctor, because our doctor no longer was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Hamburg. And she called a Dr. Stone, who later became my stepfather-in-law. And he prescribed some little medication in a little bottle. And he told me to keep this bottle in my pocketbook and give my brother some of that medication from time to time.
